title: Apollo 11 lunar landing
summary: On 20 July 1969 the American lunar module Eagle set down on the Sea of Tranquillity and, some six and a half hours later, Neil Armstrong stepped onto the surface of another world. Buzz Aldrin followed him; Michael Collins circled overhead in the command module. Apollo 11 fulfilled a promise made by John F. Kennedy in 1961, closed the most dramatic chapter of the Cold War Space Race, and remains the only occasion on which human beings have set foot on the Moon so far.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The mission grew out of a superpower contest. After the Soviet Union placed the first satellite, the first man, and the first woman in orbit, President John F. Kennedy told a joint session of Congress in May 1961 that the United States should commit itself, before the decade was out, to landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely. NASA's Apollo programme spent roughly 25 billion dollars of 1960s money and at its peak employed some 400,000 people across industry, universities, and government.

The hardware, the giant Saturn V rocket, the command and service module, and the two-stage lunar module, was proved in the Apollo 7 through Apollo 10 flights of 1968 and early 1969. The crew of Apollo 11 was announced in January 1969: Neil Armstrong, a former naval aviator and X-15 test pilot who had commanded Gemini 8, as mission commander; Air Force colonel Edwin 'Buzz' Aldrin, an MIT-trained orbital rendezvous specialist and Gemini 12 spacewalker, as lunar module pilot; and Michael Collins, veteran of Gemini 10, as command module pilot.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Apollo 11 lifted off from Launch Complex 39A at the Kennedy Space Center in Florida on 16 July 1969 at 13:32 UTC, watched by an estimated one million people along the Florida coast and hundreds of millions on television. After a translunar coast of some seventy-six hours the craft entered lunar orbit on 19 July.

On 20 July Armstrong and Aldrin transferred to the lunar module Eagle, undocked, and began their descent while Collins remained aboard the command module Columbia. The landing was not routine: an overloaded guidance computer flashed alarm codes, and Armstrong took manual control to steer past a boulder-strewn crater. Eagle touched down at 20:17 UTC in a flat basaltic plain in the Mare Tranquillitatis, with, by later estimates, less than thirty seconds of descent fuel remaining. Armstrong radioed, 'Houston, Tranquility Base here, the Eagle has landed.'

At 02:56 UTC on 21 July, some six and a half hours after touchdown, Armstrong climbed down the ladder and set his left boot in the lunar dust, saying, 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind.' Aldrin joined him nineteen minutes later. Together they spent about two hours and thirty-one minutes outside, planting an American flag and a plaque, deploying instruments including a seismometer and a laser retroreflector, and collecting about 21.5 kilograms of lunar rock and soil. President Richard Nixon spoke to them by telephone from the White House.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Eagle's ascent stage lifted off from the Moon on 21 July, rejoined Columbia, and the reunited spacecraft returned to Earth. The command module splashed down in the North Pacific on 24 July 1969 at 16:50 UTC and was recovered by the USS Hornet. The three astronauts spent three weeks in quarantine, then were greeted with parades on three continents.

Apollo 11 was followed by five further successful lunar landings through 1972, delivering ten more Americans to the surface and returning several hundred kilograms of samples that reshaped planetary science. But public and political appetite for the programme faded quickly. No human being has returned to the Moon since the crew of Apollo 17 left it in December 1972.

Beyond the geopolitical victory, the mission left cultural artefacts that endure: the photograph of Aldrin standing on the plain with Armstrong reflected in his visor; the earthrise-and-flag television footage; the plaque left behind on the descent stage, reading 'We came in peace for all mankind.' It remains the clearest single moment in which a national project became, however briefly, a human one.

worldContext.text: China was in the later phase of the Cultural Revolution launched by Mao Zedong in 1966. The Ninth Congress of the Communist Party of China (1 to 24 April 1969) formally confirmed Defence Minister Lin Biao, chief propagator of the Little Red Book, as Mao's designated successor and enshrined the doctrine of continuing revolution in a new party constitution. Red Guards had by now been demobilised through the Down to the Countryside Movement that sent perhaps 17 million urban youth to rural production brigades, while the People's Liberation Army under Lin controlled provincial revolutionary committees. In March a series of armed clashes with Soviet forces on the frozen Ussuri River at Zhenbao (Damansky) Island brought the two communist giants to the brink of open war; further clashes at Tielieketi in August led Zhou Enlai to fear a Soviet nuclear strike. The alarm helped push Beijing toward the diplomatic opening with the United States that would produce the Kissinger visit of July 1971. In Japan, Prime Minister Eisaku Sato was pressing Washington for the reversion of Okinawa, agreed in November 1969.

image.caption: Portrait of Chairman Mao Zedong on the Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen), the ubiquitous public image of the Cultural Revolution years.
image.credit: Wikimedia Commons user Wo Nai Ye Yunhe, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The Vietnam War dominated Southeast Asia. American troop strength in South Vietnam had peaked at 549,500 in April 1969, but President Richard Nixon, announcing the Nixon Doctrine of Vietnamization at Midway Island on 8 June 1969, had begun the first American troop withdrawals from South Vietnam on 8 July 1969, saying 25,000 soldiers would be gone by September. Meanwhile he had secretly extended the bombing campaign into neutral Cambodia (Operation Menu, begun 18 March 1969) under General Creighton Abrams. Preliminary peace talks in Paris under Henry Cabot Lodge, David Bruce, and later Henry Kissinger opposite Le Duc Tho continued without public result. In India, Prime Minister Indira Gandhi's Congress had split in November 1969 into the older syndicate faction and her own Congress (R); she would nationalise fourteen major banks in July. Pakistan under Yahya Khan faced the growing crisis in East Pakistan that would culminate in the December 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War. In Afghanistan, King Zahir Shah presided over a decade of constitutional experimentation.

image.caption: US Army helicopters of the 170th and 189th Assault Companies await loading of troops at Polei Kleng in the Central Highlands of South Vietnam, 10 April 1969. National Archives.

worldContext.text: The Nigerian Civil War was in its third year. The breakaway Republic of Biafra, declared by Colonel Odumegwu Ojukwu in the Igbo-majority Eastern Region on 30 May 1967 in response to the 1966 pogroms, was now blockaded by Nigerian federal forces under General Yakubu Gowon and was in the grip of a catastrophic famine that had drawn international attention. Nightly airlifts of food and medical aid organised by Joint Church Aid, the Nordic Red Cross, and Caritas flew into the Uli airstrip; the crisis prompted the founding of Medecins Sans Frontieres in December 1971 by French doctors including Bernard Kouchner who had served with the ICRC in Biafra. Some one to two million people died, mostly children of kwashiorkor. The war would end with Biafra's collapse in January 1970. Elsewhere in Africa, Libya's coup on 1 September 1969 would deepen Arab-Israeli tensions; Colonel Muammar Gaddafi in Tripoli, and Kenya was rocked in July by the assassination of Tom Mboya. Tanzania under Julius Nyerere had announced the Arusha Declaration of ujamaa socialism in 1967.

image.caption: US Centers for Disease Control workers unloading crates of codfish airlifted as relief supplies for the Nigerian civil war, 1968. The Biafran famine would provoke one of the first mass international humanitarian responses of the television age.
image.credit: CDC / Dr Lyle Conrad, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In August 1969 British troops were deployed on the streets of Belfast and Londonderry after the Battle of the Bogside (12 to 14 August), in which Catholic Bogside residents fought Royal Ulster Constabulary and loyalist attackers behind barricades, beginning Operation Banner and the thirty-year conflict known as the Troubles. Prime Minister Harold Wilson's Labour government despatched troops on 14 August at the request of Northern Ireland's Ulster Unionist Prime Minister James Chichester-Clark. A year after Warsaw Pact tanks under Marshal Ivan Yakubovsky had entered Prague on the night of 20 to 21 August 1968 and ended Alexander Dubcek's Prague Spring reforms, Czechoslovakia was under a hardening normalisation regime led from April 1969 by Gustav Husak. West German voters chose the Social Democrat Willy Brandt as chancellor in October, opening Ostpolitik outreach to the East. In France, Charles de Gaulle had resigned on 28 April after losing his referendum, and Georges Pompidou succeeded him. Spain remained under Francisco Franco, who in July designated Juan Carlos his successor.

image.caption: Soviet tanks at Hradcany Square in Prague on 21 August 1968, the day the Warsaw Pact invasion ended the Prague Spring; a year later, Czechoslovakia was under Gustav Husak's normalisation. Photograph transmitted via the Swiss embassy.
image.credit: Photograph via the Swiss embassy in Prague, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The War of Attrition between Israel and Egypt along the Suez Canal continued through 1969 in the aftermath of the June 1967 Six-Day War, in which Israeli forces had taken the Sinai, the West Bank and East Jerusalem, and the Golan Heights. Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser, humiliated in 1967, launched artillery duels across the canal, and in September 1969 Israeli commandos raided Egyptian radar installations on Green Island. On 1 September 1969 a twenty-seven-year-old signals officer named Muammar Gaddafi and a small group of Free Officers led a bloodless coup in Libya against the seventy-nine-year-old King Idris (then in Turkey for medical treatment), evicting American and British air bases, nationalising foreign oil companies, and setting the country on a revolutionary path modelled on Nasserism. In Iraq the Ba'athist regime of Ahmad Hassan al-Bakr and deputy Saddam Hussein consolidated power. In Iran the Shah's White Revolution modernisation continued amid rising Islamist opposition. Yasser Arafat had taken over the Palestine Liberation Organization in February 1969 after the previous year's Karameh battle.

image.caption: Libyan revolutionary leader Muammar Gaddafi with Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser in Cairo on 28 April 1970, six months after Gaddafi's coup deposed King Idris.
image.credit: Unknown photographer (Egyptian news archive), via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Neil Armstrong
figures.role: Mission commander; first human to walk on the Moon

figures.name: Buzz Aldrin
figures.role: Lunar module pilot; second human to walk on the Moon

figures.name: Michael Collins
figures.role: Command module pilot; remained in lunar orbit aboard Columbia while Armstrong and Aldrin descended

images.caption: Buzz Aldrin on the lunar surface near the leg of the Eagle lunar module, photographed by Neil Armstrong on 20 July 1969.
images.credit: Neil Armstrong / NASA, via Wikimedia Commons
